summary: Charity Majors argues that software engineering interviews should include operations questions, just as ops/SRE interviews now include coding questions. She contends that operational excellence is everyone's responsibility, and that interviewing for ops knowledge both identifies engineers who will own their code in production and sets cultural expectations from day one.
key_points: The industry pushes ops engineers to learn coding but doesn't equally push software engineers to learn systems and operations skills
Software engineers must be responsible for their code after deployment, not just before
Including ops questions in SWE interviews sets expectations about ownership culture from the start
The best ops interview questions are broad and open-ended, exploring experience and depth rather than pass-fail knowledge
Engineers who view ops work as lesser create toxic dynamics and poor operational outcomes
money_quotes: Our industry has gotten very good at pressing operations engineers to get better at writing code, writing tests, and software engineering in general these past few years. Which is great! But we have not been nearly so good at pushing software engineers to level up their systems skills.

You need to balance out your software engineers with engineers who don't react to every problem by writing more code. You need engineers who write code begrudgingly, as a last resort. You'll find these priceless gems in ops and SRE.

By making ops questions part of the interview, you're establishing from the start that you run an org where operations is valued, where ownership is non-optional. This is not an ivory tower where software engineers can merrily git push and go home for the day and let other people handle the fallout.
It can be toxic when you have an engineer who thinks all ops work is toil and operations engineering is lesser-than. It tends to result in operations work being done very poorly.
key_insight: Including operations questions in software engineering interviews isn't just about evaluating technical breadth — it's a cultural signal that sets expectations about code ownership and operational responsibility from the very first interaction with a candidate.
